6wCEmNYAAAAJ.keywords: Algorithms, Theoretical Computer Science, Markov chains, Mixing times, Markov chain Monte Carlo, Glauber dynamics, Sampling and counting, Statistical physics, Programmable matter, Self-organizing particle systems
6wCEmNYAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Dana Randall is a Professor of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, leading a research program at the intersection of **algorithms** and **statistical physics-inspired computation**. Her group focuses on rigorous analysis and design of randomized algorithms, with particular emphasis on <u>Markov chain</u> methods, <u>mixing time</u> analysis, and distributed stochastic processes for *self-organizing systems*. Randall’s work spans theory and applications: from foundational results in online algorithms and sampling to algorithmic models for *programmable matter* and *colloidal robotics*, she blends probabilistic techniques, combinatorial constructions, and complexity-aware algorithm design.

---

## Research Areas
Randall’s research develops and applies probabilistic and combinatorial methods for algorithmic sampling, counting, and dynamics. A central theme is the mathematical analysis of <u>Markov chains</u> — proving rapid mixing or demonstrating torpid mixing via spectral, coupling, and decomposition techniques (see e.g., “Markov chain decomposition for convergence rate analysis” (2002) and “Analyzing Glauber dynamics by comparison of Markov chains” (2000)). She has produced deep results on lattice models and tilings (“Markov chain algorithms for planar lattice structures” (1995, 2001)), and on classical statistical-mechanics models such as the Ising and hard-core models (e.g., “Sampling spin configurations of an Ising system” (1999); “Phase Coexistence and Slow Mixing for the Hard-Core Model on ℤ2” (2013)). Her interests also include randomized online algorithms and competitive analysis (“Dynamic TCP acknowledgement and other stories about e/(e-1)” (2001)), as well as emerging algorithmic models for programmable matter and robotic systems studied via stochastic local rules (“A Markov chain algorithm for compression in self-organizing particle systems” (2016); “Colloidal robotics” (2023)). Across these topics she emphasizes rigorous bounds on convergence, algorithmic implementability, and connections between discrete probability and computational practice.

## Notable Works
- “Dynamic TCP acknowledgement and other stories about e/(e-1)” (2001) — Introduced optimal randomized online algorithms for the TCP acknowledgement and related problems, extending and refining the classical ski-rental framework and yielding tight competitive guarantees for a family of online decision problems.  
- “Markov chain decomposition for convergence rate analysis” (2002) — Developed a broadly applicable toolkit for decomposing reversible Markov chains to obtain spectral gap and mixing time bounds, enabling analysis of complex chains by reducing them to easier components.  
- “Markov chain algorithms for planar lattice structures” (1995/2001) — Established algorithmic approaches and mixing-time analyses for sampling domino tilings and related planar lattice configurations, linking combinatorial tiling problems with efficient Monte Carlo sampling.

## Academic Background
Dana Randall has a long and highly cited career in **theoretical computer science** and algorithms, reflected in a publication record spanning foundational algorithmic theory to multidisciplinary work on programmable matter and robotics (total citations ~2667). She is a senior faculty member at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where she directs research at the interface of algorithms, probability, and physical models. Over decades of scholarship she has supervised students and postdocs, contributed to editorial and program-committee leadership, and obtained competitive research support to pursue both foundational questions (mixing times, sampling, counting) and novel applications (self-organizing particle systems, colloidal robotics). Her sustained influence is visible in widely used techniques for analyzing Markov chains, influential surveys synthesizing mixing-time results, and a stream of collaborative work connecting computer science to statistical physics and robotics.

72afgH4AAAAJ.keywords: Machine Learning, Systems Neuroscience, Neuromorphic Computing, Spiking Neural Networks, Neurosynaptic Hardware, Event-driven Computing, Low-power Brain-inspired Hardware, On-chip Learning, Spike-Timing-Dependent Plasticity, Address-Event Representation (AER)
72afgH4AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Nabil Imam is a researcher affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology** and **Cornell University**, whose work sits at the intersection of **machine learning** and **systems neuroscience**. He is best known for contributions to the engineering and algorithmic foundations of *neuromorphic computing*—designing brain-inspired hardware and algorithms that enable <u>energy-efficient, event-driven computation</u> with spiking neural networks. His body of work spans large-scale neurosynaptic chips, manycore neuromorphic processors with on-chip learning, and biologically inspired algorithms for rapid online learning and sensory processing. Across these efforts he emphasizes scalable communication architectures, low-power VLSI implementations, and the translation of neurobiological motifs (e.g., olfactory bulb circuits, spike-timing dynamics) into practical computing substrates.

---

## Research Areas
Imam’s research covers a blend of hardware, algorithms, and neuroscience-inspired machine learning. On the hardware side he has worked on the design and tool flows for large-scale neurosynaptic chips and digital neurosynaptic cores, addressing challenges such as hierarchical connectivity, scalable on-chip routing, and event-driven/asynchronous circuit implementations. On the algorithmic and systems-neuroscience side he has explored spiking neural network models, spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) instantiations for network traversal and learning, and biomimetic circuits for olfactory processing that enable *rapid online learning and robust recall*. He also investigates system-level tradeoffs—throughput, latency, and energy efficiency—in implementations such as manycore neuromorphic processors and million-neuron substrates, and has extended these ideas to tasks like nearest-neighbor search and active sensing with predictive coding and uncertainty minimization. Collectively his papers connect VLSI circuit techniques, communication and memory-efficient routing methods, and neuro-inspired algorithms to realize <u>scalable, low-power neural computation</u>.

---

## Notable Works
- "A million spiking-neuron integrated circuit with a scalable communication network and interface" (2014) — demonstration of a billion-plus-transistor neurosynaptic chip architecture that established scalable communication and integration strategies for large spiking networks (≈5,126 citations).  
- "Loihi: A neuromorphic manycore processor with on-chip learning" (2018) — coauthored design and functional specification of a 14 nm manycore neuromorphic processor that integrates hierarchical connectivity, dendritic compartments, synaptic delays, and on-chip learning mechanisms for SNNs (≈5,063 citations).  
- "Truenorth: Design and tool flow of a 65 mw 1 million neuron programmable neurosynaptic chip" (2015) — engineering and tool-chain exposition behind a low-power, programmable million-neuron neurosynaptic system, addressing real-time, low-energy cognitive computing constraints (≈2,273 citations).

---

## Academic Background
Imam’s publication record indicates deep, long-standing engagement with interdisciplinary teams that bring together VLSI design, computer architecture, and computational neuroscience; his work on flagship neuromorphic projects and foundational neurosynaptic cores has accumulated over **14,000 citations** across papers. He is affiliated with both **Georgia Tech** and **Cornell**, and has collaborated extensively with large-scale industry and academic neuromorphic teams (as evidenced by coauthorship on major Loihi and TrueNorth publications). His trajectory reflects training and practice across electrical engineering, computer science, and systems neuroscience; he has contributed to high-impact chip design, toolflow development, and biologically inspired learning algorithms, and is recognized in the neuromorphic community for bridging hardware implementation and neural computation.
